In a function call, use "named arguments" and put each argument on a separate line to make your code more readable. 

Here's an example of what not to do when calling the function a function `calc_fluseas_mean` (defined above):
```
mean_Y = calc_fluseas_mean(flu_data, "maari_yn", FALSE)
```

And here it is again using the best practices we've outlined:
```
mean_Y <- calc_fluseas_mean(
  data = flu_data, 
  yname = "maari_yn",
  silent = FALSE
)
```

## Function decomposition goals

Function decomposition is also an important part of readable function usage.
When a function becomes too long or handles too many concerns,
split it into smaller helper functions with clear responsibilities.

Key goals of decomposition include:

- Breaking a big problem into smaller subproblems that are easier to reason about
- Making code easier to read, review, and test
- Pushing low-level complexity "downward" into helper functions so the main workflow stays clear

## When to decompose a function

Consider decomposition when one or more of these are true:

- A function is doing multiple jobs (validation, transformation, modeling, and formatting)
- You need comments to explain every few lines just to follow the logic
- You are repeating the same block in multiple places
- You want to test one part of a workflow independently of the rest
- The top-level function is hard to scan because implementation details hide the main idea

## How to decompose in practice

A practical sequence is:

1. Write down the top-level steps in plain language.
2. Extract one step at a time into a helper function.
3. Give each helper a single, specific contract (inputs, outputs, and assumptions).
4. Keep the orchestration function short, so it reads like a workflow outline.
5. Add or update tests around each helper and the top-level function.

## Function length limits: consolidated viewpoints

The sources below combine community discussions (opinion-based)
with a formal complexity reference.
Use the community links for examples of practitioner viewpoints,
and the complexity reference for a formal metric.


- [Reddit (r/learnprogramming)](https://www.reddit.com/r/learnprogramming/comments/47avb5/one_long_function_2001000_lines_of_codes_or_one/):
  February 2016 community thread asking whether one long function
  or many smaller functions is preferable.
- [SoloLearn discussion](https://www.sololearn.com/en/Discuss/1278705/optimal-number-of-lines-of-code-of-functions):
  commenters describe different line-count conventions
  (including 20-line and 100-line limits)
  and discuss readability and testability tradeoffs.
- [Stack Overflow: "When is a function too long?"](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/475675/when-is-a-function-too-long)
  and
  [Software Engineering Stack Exchange: "What is the ideal length of a method for you?"](https://softwareengineering.stackexchange.com/questions/133404/what-is-the-ideal-length-of-a-method-for-you):
  older Q&A threads (September 2008 and February 2012, respectively)
  focused on practical criteria
  for deciding when to split methods.
- [Cyclomatic complexity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cyclomatic_complexity):
  defines complexity by counting independent execution paths/decision points;
  this reinforces that branching complexity is often a better risk indicator
  than raw line count alone.

Synthesizing these viewpoints into lab guidance:

For lab code, practical rules are:

1. Treat line count as a heuristic, not a hard constraint.
2. Prefer single-purpose functions with clear names and contracts.
3. Watch decision complexity (branching and nesting), not only length.
4. Decompose when the top-level story is hard to follow or hard to test.

## Examples borrowed from lab package PRs

In [`UCD-SERG/rpt` PR #100](https://github.com/UCD-SERG/rpt/pull/100)
(see also the [PR diff](https://github.com/UCD-SERG/rpt/pull/100.diff)),
the PR diff shows `R/example_function.R` updated to call helpers,
with dedicated helper files added (`R/validate_input.R`,
`R/clean_data.R`, `R/calculate_statistic.R`, `R/format_result.R`).
This made the top-level function easier to read,
while isolating validation and formatting details in reusable units.

In [`UCD-SERG/shigella` PR #11](https://github.com/UCD-SERG/shigella/pull/11)
(see also the [PR diff](https://github.com/UCD-SERG/shigella/pull/11.diff)),
the PR diff added focused analysis functions in separate files,
including `R/compute_residual_metrics.R`, `R/fig2_overall_newperson.R`,
`R/model_comparison_table.R`, and `R/fmt_mci.R`.
The change moved away from large script-style helpers toward
single-purpose, testable package functions.

## Related references

For background and related ideas,
see [Decomposition (computer science)](<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Decomposition_(computer_science)>),
[Code refactoring](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Code_refactoring),
and [Functional decomposition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Functional_decomposition).
